abstraction_and_responsibility.reason: The Game class appropriately orchestrates high-level game loops and state; Paddle and Ball encapsulate their own logic cohesively. However, Game handles both state management and rendering duties mixed together (e.g., draw_game_over, draw_scores embedded in run loop), and collision logic uses hardcoded threshold (10) instead of extracting boundary logic. No single point of responsibility for time handling or scoring validation beyond check_winner.

changeability_and_testability.reason: Hardcoded screen dimensions and speeds (e.g., SCREEN_WIDTH, 30 in paddle x-coords) reduce configurability. Game.run() lacks dependency injection, making unit testing hard (no way to mock screen or input). However, Game logic is split across methods (e.g., update_ai, check_collisions), and reset_game provides functional isolation. Paddle/Ball rely only on public APIs, enabling limited mockability.

comment_usefulness.reason: Comments are minimal and mostly redundant (e.g., '# 上下边界反弹' repeats obvious logic; 'AI 追踪球的位置' adds no insight). One useful comment in check_scoring describing ball out-of-bounds, but only one appears truly non-obvious or clarifying. No comments explain design intent or edge-case behavior (e.g., tie logic).

semantic_readability.reason: Method names are descriptive and consistent (e.g., update_player, check_scoring). Control flow is clear and uses early returns (draw_game_over, reset_game). Variable names (paddle_y, elapsed_time) clearly reflect intent. Minimal inline complexity (mostly simple conditionals). Only minor ambiguity: '10' in check_collisions (lines 123–132) lacks semantic meaning.
